html:
What a Real Commercial Roof Inspection Checks Beyond a Walk-Through

A lot of what gets sold as a roof inspection is someone walking the perimeter, glancing at the field, and handing over a one-page form that says "roof is in fair condition." That tells an owner almost nothing useful for budgeting, insurance, or deciding whether this year is the year to plan for replacement. A real inspection is closer to a diagnostic — measurements, photos, and a written condition assessment tied to specific locations on the roof.

The Difference Between a Walk-Through and an Inspection

A walk-through catches the obvious stuff — standing water, an obviously torn membrane, a rusted-through drain. An inspection goes further: probing seams for adhesion, checking flashing at every penetration rather than a sample, documenting membrane thickness loss from UV exposure, and noting where prior repairs have already been made and how they're holding up. We do the second one, and we show our work with photos tied to a roof plan, more than a plain written summary.

The distinction matters most when an owner is trying to decide between budgeting for repairs this year or planning a full replacement in three. A vague report doesn't support that decision. A detailed one does.

We've had owners hand us a prior year's inspection form from another company and ask what it means in practice. Most of the time there isn't much to interpret, because the form doesn't say anything specific enough to act on. A useful report should read like something a facilities manager can hand to a board or an ownership group to justify a budget line, not a checkbox exercise.

What We're Actually Looking For on a Low-Slope Roof

Membrane condition comes first — granule loss, seam integrity, blistering, and any sign of hail bruising that hasn't opened into a leak yet. Drainage is next, since ponding water ages a membrane faster than almost anything else and is often the easiest problem to actually fix. Flashing at parapets, curbs, and penetrations gets checked individually rather than assumed fine because the field looks okay. And we look at what's changed since any prior inspection, if records exist, because a membrane losing thickness at a steady rate tells you something different than one that's stable.

Rooftop equipment gets its own line in the report too. Curb flashing, condensate lines, and walk pads all interact with the membrane, and a facility with frequent HVAC service traffic tends to show wear around those units faster than the open field does. A report that lumps equipment areas in with general field condition misses where a lot of the real risk actually sits.

Post-Storm Inspections and What Documentation Actually Means

After a hail or wind event, an inspection serves a second purpose beyond routine maintenance — building the documentation an insurance claim needs. That means dated photos of hail strike patterns on the membrane, measurements of hail size where marks are visible on soft metal like pipe boots or HVAC housings, and a written record of pre-existing conditions versus new storm damage. We document what's actually there. We don't tell an owner what their claim will be worth or promise a particular outcome, because that's not something a roofing contractor can honestly say — that call belongs to the adjuster and the policy.

  • Photo documentation tied to specific roof-plan locations rather than general shots
  • Membrane thickness and granule-loss notes where UV degradation is visible
  • Drainage assessment identifying ponding areas and likely causes
  • Flashing condition at every parapet, curb, and penetration — not a sample
  • Written condition rating with remaining-life estimate rather than a simple pass or fail
  • Storm-specific notes when the inspection follows a hail or wind event

Inspection Reports That Say Nothing Useful

We've seen plenty of one-page inspection forms attached to a maintenance contract that check a box next to "satisfactory" and move on. That's not useless because it's short — it's useless because it doesn't give an owner anything to act on. Does "satisfactory" mean the roof has five years left or fifteen? Which drain is the one actually holding water? A report that can't answer that isn't really an inspection, it's a formality.

How Often a Commercial Roof Here Actually Needs a Look

Twice a year is the standard we recommend for most low-slope commercial roofs in this climate — once in spring after winter freeze-thaw and before storm season ramps up, and once in fall before winter sets in. Buildings with rooftop mechanical traffic, like distribution centers with frequent HVAC service, often benefit from an added inspection any time equipment work has happened on the roof, since that's when flashing gets disturbed most.

Roof Inspection Questions

How long does a full inspection take?

Depends on roof size, but most commercial buildings in this market take a few hours to walk, probe, and photograph thoroughly.

Do you inspect roofs you didn't install?

Yes. Most of our inspection work is on roofs we didn't originally build.

Will an inspection tell me if I have a warranty claim?

It can identify manufacturer defects versus wear or storm damage, which helps determine whether a warranty claim applies, though the manufacturer makes the final call.

Can inspection documentation help with an insurance claim?

Yes — dated photos and measurements support a claim, though we document conditions rather than predict how a claim will be decided.

What's included in a written inspection report?

Photos tied to roof locations, membrane and flashing condition notes, drainage assessment, and a remaining-life estimate.
